title: Joining Lines
subtitle: J joins the next line to the current. gJ joins without a space.
summary: J joins the line below to the current line, replacing leading whitespace with a single space (and removing comment leaders intelligently). gJ joins literally, with no whitespace fixup.

blocks.text: {key:J} joins the line below to the current line. By default it inserts a single space and trims leading whitespace from the joined line — almost always what you want for prose and code.

blocks.label: Join next line (smart: replaces whitespace with space)
Join next line (literal: no whitespace handling)

blocks.title: What J Actually Does
blocks.text:
{key:J}'s rules:

  1. Remove the trailing newline of the current line.
  2. Strip leading whitespace from the joined-in line.
  3. Strip any comment leader (`//`, `#`, `*`) if `'formatoptions'` includes `j` and the line being absorbed is in the same comment block.
  4. Insert *one space* between the joined parts (zero spaces if joining to a line that ended in `(`, or if the next line started with `)`).

{key:g}{key:J}: take *only* step 1. Pure newline removal — useful for joining lines that are intentionally adjacent without whitespace, like splitting a long URL or hex string back together.

With a count, {key:{n}}{key:J} joins n lines (the current plus n-1 below). The count is *number of lines involved*, not number of joins — so {key:2}{key:J} = {key:J} (joins one line into the current).
In Visual mode, {key:J} joins all selected lines. {key:V}-select-3-lines-{key:J} joins them all into one.
